content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS of length NNN.

In one operation, Chef can:


Select two indices iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤N,i≠j)(1 \le i, j \le N, i \ne j)(1≤i,j≤N,i=j) and flip SiS_iSi​ and SjS_jSj​. (i.e. change 000 to 111 and 111 to 000)
For example, if S=10010S = 10010S=10010 and chef applys operation on i=1i = 1i=1 and j=3j = 3j=3 then: 1‾00‾10→00110\underline{1}0\underline{0}10 \rightarrow 001101​00​10→00110.

Find if it is possible to convert SSS to a palindrome by applying the above operation **any** (possibly zero) number of times.

Note: A string is called a palindrome if it reads the same backwards and forwards, for e.g. 100011000110001 and 011001100110 are palindromic strings.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the binary string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if it is possible to convert SSS to a palindrome. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You can print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase. For example, the strings `YES`, `yes`, `Yes`, and `yEs` are all considered the same.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string. The second line contains a binary string S of length N containing only characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to transform S into a palindrome using any number of the allowed operations, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: mismatched pairs = 2 (positions (2,4) and (3,5)), even → YES.\nTest case 2: mismatched pairs = 1, N is even → NO.\nTest case 3: mismatched pairs = 1, N is odd → YES.
First case: one mismatched pair, N odd → YES. Second case: two mismatched pairs (even) → YES.
Only one mismatched pair, N even → total flips needed is odd, cannot be achieved with operations that flip two bits.

solution.approach: For each position i (0‑based) consider its mirror j = N‑1‑i. If S[i] != S[j] the pair is mismatched. To make the string a palindrome we must change exactly one character in each mismatched pair. Hence we need to flip exactly M bits, where M is the number of mismatched pairs. Each operation flips two bits, therefore the total number of flipped positions must be even. The transformation is possible iff either M is even, or M is odd but we can flip an extra neutral bit – the middle character when N is odd. Consequently the answer is YES if (M % 2 == 0) or (N % 2 == 1); otherwise NO. The algorithm scans the string once, counting mismatches, and decides in O(N) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
